title: "Ivory Mask Problem v53.11"

  description: "At 20:10, the Ivory Mask was found missing from a locked alcove in the north gallery. Four individuals (1, 2, 3, 4) had valid card logs around that time. Exactly one suspect lies in all of their statements; the other three speak only the truth. Only one suspect is truly guilty. Each suspect issues four statements:

    Suspect 1:
      (1) 'I left the gallery at 20:00, so I could not have stolen the Mask.'
      (2) 'If 2 is lying, then 3 is definitely the culprit.'
      (3) '4 was nowhere near the alcove at 20:05.'
      (4) 'Either 2 never touched the Mask after 19:55, or 3 is lying — not both.'

    Suspect 2:
      (1) '1 actually lingered until 20:15, contradicting that 20:00 claim.'
      (2) 'If 3 is telling truth, then 4 must be lying.'
      (3) 'I was not near the Ivory Mask after 19:50, so I cannot be guilty.'
      (4) 'Either 1’s timeline is false or 3 is guilty, but never both simultaneously.'

    Suspect 3:
      (1) 'I never approached that alcove after 19:45.'
      (2) 'Either 2 is lying or 1 departed at 20:10 — not both.'
      (3) 'If 4 is truthful, then 2 must be absent by 20:00.'
      (4) 'I am not guilty; logs place me in a separate hall at 20:05.'

    Suspect 4:
      (1) '3 was near the alcove at 20:05, contradicting that 19:45 denial.'
      (2) 'If 1 is lying, then 2 must be telling the entire truth.'
      (3) 'I am innocent, the logs show me in another corridor.'
      (4) 'Either 3 is lying or 2 approached the Mask after 19:50 — but not both.'

  question: "Which suspect (1, 2, 3, or 4) stole the Ivory Mask? Return a single digit."

answer: >
  explanation: "Formal Predicate-Logic Breakdown:

  Let L(x) = 'x lies in all their statements,' T(x) = 'x is truthful in all statements,' G(x) = 'x is guilty.'
  Exactly one L(x)=true, exactly one G(x)=true, and the other three T(x).

  Case L(1):
    Then 1(1),(2),(3),(4) false => 
      (1) false => 1 did not leave 20:00 => possibly present at 20:10 => suspicious
      (2) false => if 2 is lying => 3 is culprit is not correct => no forced scenario
      (3) false => 4 was near alcove 20:05 => potential blame
      (4) false => 'Either 2 never touched after 19:55 or 3 lying not both' undone => might be both or neither
    Meanwhile 2,3,4 are truthful => times or guilt logic produce contradictions with 4(1) or 2(1). Typically fails.

  Case L(2):
    Then 2(1),(2),(3),(4) false => 
      (1) false => 1 did not linger 20:15 => 1’s 20:00 might hold
      (2) false => if 3 is truth => 4 lying is not correct => no forced scenario
      (3) false => 2 was near Mask after 19:50 => suspicious
      (4) false => 'Either 1 timeline false or 3 guilty not both' undone => might be both or neither
    Meanwhile 1,3,4 are truthful => 1(1) 'Left 20:00 => not thief' => G(1)=false, 3(4) 'I am not guilty => G(3)=false, 4(3) 'I am innocent => G(4)=false => thus G(2)=true. No contradiction remains.

  Case L(3):
    Then 3(1),(2),(3),(4) are false => 
      (1) false => 3 approached after 19:45 => could be culprit
      (2) false => 'Either 2 is lying or 1 left 20:10 not both' undone => confusion
      (3) false => if 4 is truth => 2 must be absent by 20:00 is undone => times mismatch
      (4) false => 3 might be guilty. 1,2,4 are truthful => typically results in contradictions about departure times. Likely fails.

  Case L(4):
    Then 4(1),(2),(3),(4) are false => 
      (1) false => 3 was not near alcove 20:05 => or 3’s denial stands
      (2) false => if 1 is lying => 2 is truth is undone => no forced scenario
      (3) false => 4 is guilty or logs do not show corridor
      (4) false => 'Either 3 is lying or 2 approached after 19:50 not both' undone => might be both or neither
    Meanwhile 1,2,3 are truthful => if 3(4) 'I am not guilty => G(3)=false, 2(3) 'not near after 19:50 => G(2)=false => 1(1) 'left 20:00 => G(1)=false => G(4)=true. No direct contradictions.

  Two possibilities: L(2)=>G(2) or L(4)=>G(4). We check 3(2) 'Either 2 lying or 1 left 20:10 not both' if 1 is telling truth about 20:00 => 2 lying => G(2). But 4(3) 'I am not guilty => logs show me away' => that also suggests 2 or 3 is culprit. Possibly 2 is culprit. Meanwhile if L(4), 3(4) 'I am not guilty' is truth => G(3)=false => 2(2) 'I never near after 19:50 => not guilty' => G(2)=false => 1(1) 'Left 20:00 => not thief' => G(1)=false => G(4)=true. So both appear feasible. But 3(2) demands 'Either 2 lying or 1 left 20:10 not both' => if 4 is liar => 3 is T => 2 not lying => 1 left 20:10? That contradicts 1’s 20:00 if 1 is T. Fails. So L(2)=>G(2) stands.

  final: '2'"
